title: De-embedding intrinsic terahertz spectral signatures of RDX, TNT, and CL-20 from granular soil matrices via effective medium theory.
abstract: The detection of energetic materials concealed within complex, heterogeneous environments such as soil remains a critical challenge in defense and security applications. Granular soil matrices introduce significant scattering and background absorption in the terahertz (THz) regime, often obscuring the characteristic spectral fingerprints of concealed threats. This study employs Terahertz Time-Domain Spectroscopy (THz-TDS) in transmission mode (0.1-3.0 THz) to interrogate mixtures of soil with three high-energy explosives: RDX, TNT, and CL-20. Despite the high-loss environment characterized by strong Mie/Rayleigh scattering and water vapor absorption lines, distinct spectral features of the explosives were experimentally resolved. To quantitatively recover the intrinsic dielectric properties of the energetic materials from the effective medium response, three Effective Medium Theory (EMT) models-Maxwell-Garnett (MG), Bruggeman (BG), and Landau-Lifshitz-Looyenga (LLL)-were numerically inverted and applied. We demonstrate that these models can successfully de-embed the complex refractive index and absorption coefficient of the pure explosives from the soil-explosive composite data. The analysis reveals that while all models show high consistency in recovering the frequency positions of characteristic phonon modes (e.g., 0.82 THz for RDX, 1.60 THz for TNT), they exhibit distinct behaviours in amplitude estimation, particularly for materials with complex vibrational manifolds like TNT. This work establishes a robust methodological framework for the computational extraction of threat signatures from highly scattering granular media without reliance on reference libraries of mixtures.
journal: Spectrochim Acta A Mol Biomol Spectrosc
